statistical_assertions.text: In Q4 2024, Taiwan Semiconductor Manufacturing Corporation (TSMC) accounted for 71% of the global semiconductor foundry revenue, a number that “far outpaces second-place Samsung, which accounts for 8% of global chip manufacturing revenue” (Caporal).
The largest American semiconductor foundry, GlobalFoundries, only accounts for 4% of global revenue according to the same list (Caporal).

Market research conducted by Clair Brown, Professor of Economics and Director at the Center for Work, Technology and Society at the University of California, Berkeley, showed that in 1980, RCA was not even in the top 10 largest semiconductor firms (18).
The number one spot in 1980 belonged to Texas Instruments, which Brown showed held a 14% share in a $9.4 billion market (18).

A Yahoo Finance article notes that Morgan Stanley forecasted that Semiconductor Manufacturing International Corporation, China’s largest semiconductor foundry, would see their “7-nanometer and below capacity to nearly double” from 2025 to 2026, and although this technology is still a few generations behind TMSC, continued government investment into China’s homegrown semiconductor industry paints a similar picture to TSMC’s own past.

resolved_citations.raw_text: Brown, Clair, and Greg Linden. Chips and Change: How Crisis Reshapes the Semiconductor Industry. The MIT Press, 2009. direct.mit.edu, https://doi.org/10.7551/mitpress/9780262013468.001.0001.
resolved_citations.title: Chips and Change: How Crisis Reshapes the Semiconductor Industry
resolved_citations.source_text: For decades the semiconductor industry has been a driver of global economic growth and social change. Semiconductors, particularly the microchips essential to most electronic devices, have transformed computing, communications, entertainment, and industry. In Chips and Change, Clair Brown and Greg Linden trace the industry over more than twenty years through eight technical and competitive crises that forced it to adapt in order to continue its exponential rate of improved chip performance. The industry's changes have in turn shifted the basis on which firms hold or gain global competitive advantage. These eight interrelated crises do not have tidy beginnings and ends. Most, in fact, are still ongoing, often in altered form. The U.S. semiconductor industry's fear that it would be overtaken by Japan in the 1980s, for example, foreshadows current concerns over the new global competitors China and India. The intersecting crises of rising costs for both design and manufacturing are compounded by consumer pressure for lower prices. Other crises discussed in the book include the industry's steady march toward the limits of physics, the fierce competition that keeps its profits modest even as development costs soar, and the global search for engineering talent. Other high-tech industries face crises of their own, and the semiconductor industry has much to teach about how industries are transformed in response to such powerful forces as technological change, shifting product markets, and globalization. Chips and Change also offers insights into how chip firms have developed, defended, and, in some cases, lost global competitive advantage.

resolved_citations.raw_text: Coble, Parks M. The Collapse of Nationalist China: How Chiang Kai-Shek Lost China’s Civil War. Cambridge University Press, 2023. Cambridge University Press, https://doi.org/10.1017/9781009297639.
resolved_citations.title: The Collapse of Nationalist China: How Chiang Kai-Shek Lost China’s Civil War
resolved_citations.source_text: When World War II ended Chiang Kai-shek seemed at the height of his power-the leader of Nationalist China, one of the victorious Allied Powers in 1945 and with the financial backing of the US. Yet less than four years later, he lost the China's civil war against the communists. Offering an insightful chronological treatment of the years 1944–1949, Parks Coble addresses why Chiang was unable to win the war and control hyperinflation. Using newly available archival sources, he reveals the critical weakness of Chiang's style of governing, the fundamental structural flaws in the Nationalist government, bitter personal rivalries and Chiang's personal lack of interest in finance. This major work of revisionist scholarship will engage all those interested in the shaping of twentieth-century history.

resolved_citations.raw_text: Moore, Gordon E. Cramming More Components onto Integrated Circuits.
resolved_citations.title: Cramming More Components onto Integrated Circuits
resolved_citations.source_text: The future of integrated electronics is the future of electronics itself. The advantages of integration will bring about a proliferation of electronics, pushing this science into many new areas. Integrated circuits will lead to such wonders as home computers—or at least terminals connected to a central computer—automatic controls for automobiles, and personal portable communications equipment. The electronic wristwatch needs only a display to be feasible today. But the biggest potential lies in the production of large systems. In telephone communications, integrated circuits in digital filters will separate channels on multiplex equipment. Integrated circuits will also switch telephone circuits and perform data processing. Computers will be more powerful, and will be organized in completely different ways. For example, memories built of integrated electronics may be distributed throughout the machine instead of being concentrated in a central unit. In addition, the improved reliability made possible by integrated circuits will allow the construction of larger processing units. Machines similar to those in existence today will be built at lower costs and with faster turnaround.

resolved_citations.source_text: By 1916, it had become apparent that the United States, despite having invented the airplane, was lagging behind several European countries in developing an aircraft industry.' Fearing the potentially negative consequences of such a development for both the commercial and the strategic interests of the United States, Congress, and the president joined together in 1916 to create the National Advisory Committee on Aeronautics, better known as NACA.

paper_text:
Liu 1
Stanford University
Ask What Your Country Can Do for Taiwan Semiconductor
Kenneth Liu
PWR 1: Imagining Technology
Dr. Meg Formato
17 Mar. 2026

Liu 2
Abstract
Taiwan Semiconductor Manufacturing Company Limited has established a virtual monopoly
over the semiconductor foundry industry, yet little research has been conducted about how the
geopolitical state of Taiwan in the 1980s fueled TSMC’s growth. This paper first takes a
historical look into how the formation of Taiwan as a state governed by the Nationalists resulted
from America’s hesitancy to fully support the Nationalists during the Chinese Civil War and how
that affected the structure of Taiwan’s society and industry, creating conditions favorable for the
development of a local semiconductor industry. Then, this paper will describe how the hesitancy
of American microchip firms to share designs with competing Taiwanese semiconductor
foundries catalyzed the formation of a “pure-play” foundry model that enabled TSMC to perfect
its manufacturing processes, eventually exceeding the quality and cost-effectiveness of American
foundries. America’s loss of superiority in the semiconductor manufacturing industry as
discussed in this paper has broad implications for America’s stance regarding microchip design
in the current tensions with China if America wishes to remain ahead in this area.

Liu 3
Semiconductors are unique materials in that they selectively transmit or block electrical
signals. This property makes them a natural choice for miniature electronic circuits, which is
why semiconductors (in particular silicon semiconductors) are the basic unit for modern
microchips. Today, the size of the transistors (the smallest computational units of a chip) measure
on the scale of nanometers; any smaller, and quantum physics makes the chips unreliable. Due to
the small size of these components, the process of converting raw silicon into computer chips
capable of running billions or trillions of operations a second has required massive global
collaboration and innovation.
Furthermore, the semiconductor industry progresses at breakneck speeds. A commonly
cited principle in the industry is “Moore’s Law”. In 1965, Gordon Moore, who would go on to
co-found Intel Corporation, predicted that the number of components in a microchip would
double approximately every year (83). And according to ASML, the leading manufacturer of
semiconductor foundry equipment and a major supplier of TSMC, although he revised it to every
2 years in 1975, this prophecy has held true, as transistor count “has grown from tens in the
1960s to tens of billions today”. The rapid advancement of the field makes it difficult for
newcomers to keep up with established players in semiconductor design and manufacturing,
limiting advanced microchip production capabilities to a few countries.
The difficulty of manufacturing microchips coupled with the fast product cycles raises
the question of how Taiwan was able to establish such a strong hold over the industry, despite
starting decades behind the United States and Japan. In fact, a statistical report written for The
Motley Fool, a leading financial advice company, drew data from TrendForce and noted that in
Q4 2024, Taiwan Semiconductor Manufacturing Corporation (TSMC) accounted for 71% of the
global semiconductor foundry revenue, a number that “far outpaces second-place Samsung,
which accounts for 8% of global chip manufacturing revenue” (Caporal). The largest American
semiconductor foundry, GlobalFoundries, only accounts for 4% of global revenue according to
the same list (Caporal). Taiwan Semiconductor Manufacturing Corporation (TSMC) produces
most cutting-edge chips, ranging from the Apple Silicon chips in Apple phones and computers to
data-center ready Nvida GPUs powering the next large language models.
In this paper, we’ll discuss how Taiwan was able to make such a meteoric rise from its
founding in 1987 through the so-called “pure-play” foundry model. Many leading chip designers

Liu 4
such as AMD, Apple, Nvidia, and ARM in the US do not have their own foundries, instead
relying on TSMC,  and other "pure-play" foundries that don't design their own chips to
manufacture designs. Apple has contracted TSMC for manufacturing its iPhone chips since the
iPhone 6 in 2014, and switched to TSMC to design in-house laptop chips starting with the
Macbook M1 in 2029. The “pure-play” foundry  strategy contrasts with the strategy of vertically
integrated semiconductor companies like Samsung, who would design and manufacture their
own chips.
TSMC's dominance in the semiconductor foundry industry was the result of years of
government investment into innovation and a combination of geopolitical factors that ultimately
powered Taiwan's jump from a Third World country assembling electronics for other companies
to leading the bleeding edge of chip manufacturing. I will argue that it’s not necessarily the US’s
direct support of Taiwan that allowed TSMC to become dominant, but America's hesitancy to
become involved fully that required Taiwan to innovate and surpass the US.
First, I'll argue that America's hesitancy to support the Nationalist government during the
second phase of the Chinese Civil War from 1945-1950 or a Taiwan independent from the
Nationalists led to a two-tier society within Taiwan, with Nationalists originally from the
mainland dominating native islanders in politics and industry. Furthermore, the ephemeral nature
of America’s support for Taiwan motivated Taiwan to develop a semiconductor industry that the
United States would be compelled to protect out of self-interest.
This in turn created an environment through the 1990s where two tiers of semiconductor
industry companies co-existed, with smaller firms owned by local Taiwanese and larger firms
owned by mainlanders and supported by the Nationalist government. This benefited TSMC,
since American firms were often hesitant to provide their chip designs to TSMC. The dichotomy
would enable TSMC to perfect their "pure-play" foundry through small-scale contracts with local
Taiwanese firms, before gaining sufficient traction to attract larger American companies like
Apple to manufacture their chips.
It’s important for America to understand the interdependence of the global system.
Limiting the access that other countries have to technology or knowledge doesn’t prevent them

Liu 5
from accessing it, and as in the case of Taiwan, other countries may even eventually surpass
America in technical capability.
The Chinese Civil War and Nationalist Influence on Taiwan
To understand the formation of Taiwan’s semiconductor industry, it’s important to first
understand the formation of Taiwan as a nation. Steven E. Phillip’s dissertation Between
Assimilation and Independence: The Taiwanese Encounter Nationalist China, 1945-1950, tells
us that Taiwan, which had been occupied during the Second World War by Japan, became a
province under the Chinese Nationalist government in 1945 and was inhabited by a combination
of native residents and Han Chinese migrants (40). The Communists and Nationalists, who had
been fighting since the 1920s and had created a joint effort for the Second World War against the
Japanese, resumed fighting from 1945 (Phillips 53). During this time, the Nationalist government
was located in Nanjing, led by Jiang Jieshi, while the Communist government was in Beijing
(Phillips 1). The United States initially supported the Nationalist government in this conflict to
curb the spread of communism, providing soldiers to shore up their defenses.
Parks Coble, a Professor of History specializing in 20th century China, notes that
hyperinflation ravaged Nationalist China during the Civil War, and “morale among both the
civilian and military components of the Chiang regime fell steadily, as did its international
prestige” (41).  He points out that the hyperinflation was caused by corruption from the highest
levels down to military officers (203). Furthermore, Coble highlights American intelligence
reports that “thought that the spending for the military by the Nationalist government was
substantially more than was needed for the size of its army” since “officers and regional
militarists pilfered much of their money and supplies” (204). As a result, the US was hesitant to
provide further financial or military support to the Nationalists, accelerating the collapse of their
hold on the mainland.
In 1948, General Wei, the governor of the province of Taiwan who, according to Steven
Phillips, was “frustrated by his lack of real power,” extended an offer to the United States to
pursue a Taiwan independent of the Nationalist government on the mainland (95). Phillips notes
that doubts about whether Wei could successfully establish an independent government led the
US to continue supporting the Nationalist government in Nanjing (96). By hesitating to accept
this offer, the Nationalist government was able to replace Wei with another, General Chen

Liu 6
Cheng, who was more aligned with the central Nationalist government, ending the
natives-friendly policies that Wei had established as well as any hopes for independence(96).
The Formation of a Two-Tier Taiwan
The frontline conflict occurring throughout the Chinese mainland led to millions of
Chinese citizens seeking refuge in Taiwan. If Taiwan had been able to gain their independence
from the Nationalist government, this would have left Taiwan with a much more local-heavy
population, and therefore a more local-sympathetic government. But because Taiwanese
independe never reached independence, more and more mainlander Nationalist residents fled the
mainland to Taiwan while the Nationalist army lost ground. The US’s withdrawal from the
region ensured the collapse of the Nationalist government on the mainland, creating a dichotomy
on the island between mainlander refugees and locals.
Due to the colonization of Taiwan by the Japanese during the Second World War, the
Nationalist government believed that the natives had built up “Japanese identity and loyalty to
the Japanese emperor”, notes Kuo-Hui Chang, an associate professor of national development at
National Taiwan University, in his book Engineers and the Two Taiwans, justifying the
government’s stance that “their university degrees marked their knowledge as far superior to
anything held by the vast majority” of the native workers (52). As a result, a two-tier society
formed, with mainlanders attending better schools and pursuing better careers than those from
the island. For instance, according to Chang, a majority of the students at Taida, the premier
engineering university in Taiwan, were from mainland families (3). For decades, the policy of
the Nationalist government was that they would eventually retake the Chinese mainland. As a
result, Chang notes that mainlanders did not hold strong attachments to Taiwan and were more
willing to study abroad or relocate permanently to the United States (3).
Governmental Investment for Mainlanders
​
In the post-war era, Chang describes how the “technological teaching and industrial
development” through the nearly restored National Chiao Tung University of Electronics was
“aimed to protect and promote ‘Free China’” on the island of Taiwan (73-4). Taiwan felt a strong
need to demonstrate that they could keep up with the communists on the mainland in economic
and military development, and electronics was a key part of this nonconfrontational conflict.

Liu 7
Chang notes that this pressure was particularly acute because the US announced an end to
foreign aid to the country in 1965, necessitating the development of robust local industry (75). In
addition, when the US withdrew recognition of the Republic of China in Taiwan as the
government of China and established diplomatic relations with the People’s Republic of China
on the mainland in 1979, American assistance in the event of an invasion of the island became
even less sure (China - Countries - Office of the Historian). Taiwan recognized the potential of
semiconductors to act as a powerful balancer in its geopolitical relations with the significantly
larger People’s Republic of China and United States, prompting large-scale government
investment into semiconductors.
​
As a result, the Nationalist government also played a role in expanding the semiconductor
industry by assisting in financing several semiconductor companies/initatives that would
primarily benefit mainlanders. Kuo-Hui Chang notes that Sun Yun-Suan, and 30 other
mainlanders had been dispatched by the Nationalist government to receive training in America
with the purpose of developing Taiwan’s industry, as Sun Yun-Suan would go on to establish the
Industrial Technology Research Institute, which would go on to spin off several companies like
TSMC and one of TSMC’s main competitors, United Microelectronics Corporation (79). Chang
notes that UMC was primarily populated with mainlander engineers (83).
​
The two-tier society that developed in Taiwan following the retreat of the Nationalist
government was also seen within the structure of the semiconductor industry on the island.
Contrasting the companies supported by the Nationalist government, William E. Newburry, an
Industrial and Domestic Contracts Administrator at McDonnell Douglas, noted that there existed
a trend of small Taiwanese companies because many men desired to “become ‘President’ or
‘Chairman’ of one’s own corporation” that produced a “highly-competitive business
environment” (157). These firms tended to be owned by islander natives, who were unable to
gain admission into the most prestigious universities on the island and travel abroad (Chang et al.
4).
Unique Strengths of the Two-Tier System
The vast number of small Taiwanese firms founded by islanders would provide valuable
business to TSMC. Opening a semiconductor foundry of their own was a financial impossibility
for most small Taiwanese firms; instead, these firms would turn to pure-play foundries, most

Liu 8
notably TSMC, to manufacture their integrated circuits. This lowered the barrier to entry to
semiconductors as firms could focus on the design and marketing processes rather than
manufacturing. And in the 1990s, Annalee Saxenian, an observer of the semiconductor industry
in Taiwan, noted that the industry consisted of “10,000 ‘highly specialized’ producers” while
Kuo-Hui Chang adds that the “vast majority…were founded by islanders” (91). The growing
number of contracts with local Taiwanese companies provided TSMC with the capital to
continue perfecting its fabrication processes. This is because American firms were often
unwilling to do business with Taiwanese foundries, at least initially, when Taiwanese foundries
were less sophisticated than American ones.
Furthermore, the two-tier society present in Taiwan enabled the coexistence of skilled
managers and low-skill cheap labor crucial for the development of local semiconductor
industries. The mainlanders often felt less attached to the island and had greater financial means,
so they were often able to immigrate to the United States for education and work. On request,
some would return to Taiwan to develop the local industry, where they would take up higher
level positions due to their education and work experience within the US’s more mature
semicondcturo industry. At the same time, natives with less opportunity to pursue higher
education were more willing to settle for lower wages and worse workign conditions, especially
due to the presence of immigrants, working in semiconductor foundries and making the cost of
production competitive compared to the United States (Newburry 164).
US Fears of Intellectual Property Theft
America in the 1980s was hesitant to share its leading chip designs with foreign
manufacturers for a variety of reasons. Culturally, there was a sentiment that manufacturing was
important to America and should not be offshored. For instance, in 1992, Clyde V Prestowitz,
former Secretary of Commerce under Ronald Reagan, criticized the pending decision to sell 40
percent of McDonnell Douglas, an aerospace manufacturing company, to Taiwan (483). He
compared the stagnation that America faced to the reconfirmation of America’s dedication to
world leadership in the aerospace industry through President John F. Kennedy’s 1960 call to “put
a man on the moon by the end of the decade” (482-483). In this speech, Kennedy argued that
humanity will go to the moon and do other things “not because they are easy, but because they
are hard, because that goal will serve to organize and measure the best of our energies and

Liu 9
skills”. Prestowitz criticized America’s willingness to relinquish leadership in the aerospace
industry by divesting their hard-earned intellectual property.
A similar pushback happened in the semiconductor industry. Of the American
semiconductor firms, only RCA (Radio Corporation of America) was willing to work with ITRI
to produce semiconductors. The technology from RCA that ITRI licensed in 1975, was
according to Morris Chang,  “a generation behind the leading technology in the US” (“Interview
with Morris Chang, 2007 August 24” 0:41:10). Market research conducted by Clair Brown,
Professor of Economics and Director at the Center for Work, Technology and Society at the
University of California, Berkeley, showed that in 1980, RCA was not even in the top 10 largest
semiconductor firms (18). The number one spot in 1980 belonged to Texas Instruments, which
Brown showed held a 14% share in a $9.4 billion market (18). In addition, the chairman of Texas
Instruments in 1992, Jeryy Junkins, confirmed “that Texas Instruments will only license its
1-megabit chip design when the company is ready to advance into the 4-megabit DRAM level”
(Newburry 169). Finally, Shien-Yang Wu, Vice President of Advanced Technology Development
at TSMC, cites that Phillips, which had partnered as a joint venture partner in TSMC alongside
the Taiwanese government transferred their VLSI technology on the 2 micrometer resolution to
TSMC in 1987, which trailed behind the 1 micrometer world best (449). Despite some
willingness among American firms like Texas Instruments to invest in TSMC, none were willing
to transfer the cutting edge echnology they possessed to a foreign power.
Debut of the “Pure-play” Foundry and Eventual Growth
​
By continuing to participate in limited contracts with American firms and Taiwanese
firms, TSMC gradually built up a reputation for excellence. Particularly because TSMC did not
create its own chip designs and as a consequence did not compete with its customers, they
became more receptive to partnering with TSMC as time passed. Shien-Yang Wu marked the
period of 1987 to 1990 as TSMC’s “embryonic stage” where they debuted the new “pure-play”
foundry model (443). Wu cites how 90% of TSMC’s business came from companies who didn’t
have sufficient semiconductor manufacturing capacity or were offloading production of outdated
product lines (443). However, the advantages afforded by the “pure-play” foundry model were
undeniable. TSMC claimed no competitive threat to any of its customers, since they didn’t
actively design any chips. Furthermore, by focusing exclusively on manufacturing, they were
able to invest research and development efforts on this process; Wu highlights how TSMC was

Liu 10
“one of the first wafer fabrication plants all around the world to install SMIF process
technology” which helped improve yields and increased the speed at which fabs could be built
(443). Because of TSMC’s unique manufacturing-only focus, they have been able to invest more
in research and development compared to more vertically integrated companies.
Relevance to the Present Day
​
Within the span of a few decades, America went from essentially the sole player in the
semiconductor industry to outsourcing much of its manufacturing to Asia, and specifically
TSMC for cutting edge chips. By the year 2000, Craig Addison, a semiconductors expert writing
for the New York Times, described how a “silicon shield” would make China hesitate to launch a
large scale invasion of the island due to the possibility destroying a semiconductor fabrication
plant during the fighting, which would cripple the global computer supply chain. It turns out,
Taiwan’s big gamble on semiconductors has paid off, giving Taiwan some level of national
security despite the threat of invasion.
The relevance of chip supremacy, as first identified by Taiwan, has only strengthened
within the last few years. The advent of advanced computer vision models and generative/agentic
artificial intelligence has empowered countries with advanced microchip capabilities to train
models with direct militarial applications. The use of Claude by the Pentagon, as noted by Tara
Copp in a recent Washington Post article, has enabled the U.S. military to “strike a blistering
1,000 targets in the first 24 hours of its attack on Iran”. Claude, a product of the leading AI lab
Anthropic, was trained on Nvidia GPUs which originated within a TSMC plant (Microsoft,
NVIDIA and Anthropic Announced New Strategic Partnerships.). As such, the United States has
a direct militarial responsibility to ensure that it retains access to these chips so that new models
can be trained and utilized in the field. This gives countries like Taiwan a significant bargaining
chip against the US.
​
Karen Sutter, a Specialist in Asian Trade and Finance writes in a Library of Congress
report that due to the military implications of advanced artificial intelligence, the US is currently
attempting to restrict the imports of advanced chips and the technology required to produce
advanced chips to China in the hopes of limiting the development of artificial intelligence there.
However, there are already beginning signs that China is circumventing America’s attempts,
developing its own homegrown technologies that may one day surpass the US. A Yahoo Finance
article notes that Morgan Stanley forecasted that Semiconductor Manufacturing International

Liu 11
Corporation, China’s largest semiconductor foundry, would see their “7-nanometer and below
capacity to nearly double” from 2025 to 2026, and although this technology is still a few
generations behind TMSC, continued government investment into China’s homegrown
semiconductor industry paints a similar picture to TSMC’s own past.

Reflective Memo
I made a significant structural change between my draft and revision by first describing a
specific feature of the US-Taiwan relationship and then how it affected TSMC, then proceeding
to the next feature, rather than attempting to first describe all the features in the Taiwan-US
relationship, and then systematically describing how each led to the development of TSMC. I
feel that this revision makes the structure of my paper much easier to follow.
Another change I made was to substantiate more of my broad claims about the
semiconductor industry and the historical context of Taiwan-US relations with citations. This
was not a priority during my draft, but I believe that it has made a large difference for my
revision.
If I had more time with the research project, I definitely would have extensively utilized
the Computer History Museum and the extensive video recording archives in the Stanford Media
Center. I could have also dedicated some effort to translate original documents from TSMC
written in Chinese, as I would gain more insight into the perspective of Nationalist government
officials or insiders at TSMC.
I think the most significant learning from this research project is just how interrelated
technological development is. Behind the technological advancements occurring at companies
like TSMC are thousands of individuals, motivated not just by the need for technological
progress but also geopolitical stability. I have also learned how to make better use of textual
evidence by using signal phrases as needed to engage my paper in a broader conversation.
title: Liu 1 Stanford University Ask What Your Country Can Do for Taiwan Semiconductor
